narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which actually stands out above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model designed to forecast first-time bar exam success based on students' academic records, family backgrounds, and other factors from past data. The model assessed your profile and predicted a 44.5% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, a risk level high enough to trigger the rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes. Among all the elements in your background, three stood out as having the strongest sway on this decision: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (well below the average of 5.53), and your Year 1 school decile ranking of 2 (compared to the typical 5.71). Your undergraduate GPA was the most influential factor overall, working strongly in your favor by signaling to the model a higher likelihood of passing the bar, as it exceeded what most applicants bring. In contrast, your lower Year 3 decile ranking pushed the prediction toward failure more than any other element, suggesting to the model that your performance relative to peers in that later year raised concerns about sustaining success through law school and beyond. Your Year 1 decile ranking also nudged the outlook toward failure, though with somewhat less force, reinforcing worries about early academic standing in a competitive school environment. These decile positions highlight how your grades placed you in the lower ranks compared to typical students, overshadowing strengths like your middle family income bracket and full-time study plans, which align with the vast majority of applicants. While your LSAT score of 30.50 falls short of the 36.70 average, it wasn't among the top drivers here. This combination tipped the scales just enough for the model to flag you as a higher-risk admit. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or context in a new application, and gathering letters that highlight your undergraduate success and resilience as a dedicated full-time student. Many applicants in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps in subsequent cycles, so revisiting your prep strategy could make a real difference next time.
